module.label: Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen

use_when: Infant, child, or adolescent with abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, suspected appendicitis, gastroenteritis, UTI-related abdominal symptoms, or possible acute abdomen.

Pediatric abdominal or vomiting workup needs baseline safety data, age-band routing, dehydration assessment, surgical red flags, pregnancy-capable adolescent routing, and source-backed imaging/testing choices.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use adult abdominal pain, pelvic pain, GU, renal, or infection workup as appropriate.
Unstable child with shock, peritonitis, bilious vomiting, suspected obstruction, testicular torsion, or possible ectopic pregnancy; escalate immediately while documenting the focused workup.
Primary fever or suspected sepsis without abdominal, vomiting, diarrheal, urinary, pelvic, or scrotal symptoms; use pediatric fever, infection, or sepsis pathway.
Pregnant or recently postpartum adolescent with systemic illness or obstetric concern; add obstetric emergency and pregnancy-specific escalation guidance.

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric abdominal age band
requiredQuestions.text: Which age band applies: under 6 weeks, 6 weeks to under 3 months, 3 to 11 months, 12 to 23 months, 2 to 4 years, 5 to 11 years, or 12 to 15 years?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Age changes appendicitis presentation, dehydration risk, urine testing approach, pregnancy applicability, and neonatal/infant escalation.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Age band changes differential, imaging strategy, urine collection route, ORS risk, febrile infant routing, transfer threshold, and adolescent confidential history.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain assessment and age-sensitive differential diagnosis
source.implementation_notes: Use exact age to prevent adult pathway leakage and to decide adolescent pregnancy/scrotal/gynecologic routing.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to age-band eligibility; it determines pathway scope and risk interpretation.

requiredQuestions.label: Pain location and trajectory
requiredQuestions.text: Where is the pain now, where did it start, has it migrated, and is it becoming localized, constant, severe, or worse with movement?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every child with abdominal pain or vomiting where pain may be present but under-described.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies appendicitis, peritonitis, obstruction, pelvic pathology, renal/urinary source, and need for repeated exam.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Localized, migrating, progressive, or movement-related pain changes surgical consultation, imaging, analgesia, observation, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: history features, repeated examination, and investigation guided by likely cause
source.implementation_notes: Document denied and endorsed pain features separately; do not bundle into generic abdominal history.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available here; use trajectory and localization to trigger source-backed surgical and imaging pathways.

requiredQuestions.label: Vomiting character
requiredQuestions.text: Is vomiting bilious green, bloody, persistent without diarrhea, projectile, early-morning with headache, or associated with abdominal distension?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every vomiting child and when abdominal pain is accompanied by nausea, poor intake, or dehydration.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates gastroenteritis from obstruction, intracranial cause, sepsis, surgical abdomen, and other dangerous mimics.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Bilious, bloody, persistent, distension-associated, or neurologic-pattern vomiting changes emergency referral, imaging, fluids, observation, and specialty consultation.

source.source_section: vomiting key points and serious alternative diagnoses
source.implementation_notes: Bilious green vomiting should be treated as obstruction until proven otherwise.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this safety screen; guideline red flags determine escalation.

requiredQuestions.label: Diarrhea stool and exposure features
requiredQuestions.text: Are there loose or watery stools, blood or mucus in stool, travel, outbreak exposure, sick contacts, food exposure, or no improvement by day 7?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting, diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, or dehydration could represent gastroenteritis or enteric infection.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes likely gastroenteritis from alternative diagnoses and identifies stool microbiology or public-health triggers.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Blood/mucus, travel, outbreak, immunocompromise, suspected septicemia, or prolonged illness changes stool testing, isolation/public health, antibiotics consideration, and follow-up.

source.source_section: diagnosis of gastroenteritis and stool microbiology indications
source.implementation_notes: Use multi-select documentation because more than one exposure or stool feature can be true.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this stool and exposure item; positive features trigger guideline-based testing and public-health steps.

requiredQuestions.label: Dehydration risk history
requiredQuestions.text: Has the child had reduced intake, fewer wet nappies or less urine, more than 5 diarrheal stools in 24 hours, more than 2 vomits in 24 hours, inability to keep fluids down, age under 1 year, or low birth weight?
options: more than 5 diarrheal stools in 24 hours
age under 1 year or low birth weight
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting, diarrhea, poor intake, fever, or abdominal pain could cause dehydration.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies dehydration risk and shock trajectory before discharge or oral rehydration planning.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive risk factors change ORS use, observation, rehydration route, reassessment timing, admission threshold, and home safety-netting.

source.source_section: risk factors for dehydration and clinical dehydration/shock assessment
source.implementation_notes: This is explicitly multi-select; each endorsed risk changes hydration and disposition.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this combined dehydration risk item; use guideline risk factors and bedside hydration findings.

requiredQuestions.label: Urinary symptom cluster
requiredQuestions.text: Are dysuria, urinary frequency, new bedwetting, malodorous urine, dark or cloudy urine, visible hematuria, flank pain, suprapubic pain, fever or shivering, prior UTI, or reduced intake present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, nonspecific illness, or urinary concern in children and young people.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for UTI or pyelonephritis symptoms that can present as abdominal pain, vomiting, or fever.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Endorsed urinary symptoms change urinalysis/culture, antibiotics, referral, follow-up, and constipation/dysfunctional elimination management.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs that increase likelihood of UTI in babies, children, and young people
source.implementation_notes: Use multi-select positive/negative documentation; urinary features should not be hidden inside generic abdominal history.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this urinary symptom cluster; NICE symptom groups and urine testing guide management.

requiredQuestions.label: Adolescent pregnancy context
requiredQuestions.text: For a postmenarchal or pregnancy-capable adolescent, is pregnancy possible, is there missed or late period, vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, syncope, shoulder-tip pain, rectal pressure, or prior ectopic pregnancy?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when the patient is postmenarchal, pregnancy-capable, has lower abdominal/pelvic pain, vomiting, syncope, bleeding, or pregnancy possibility.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pregnancy or ectopic pregnancy risk before attributing adolescent lower abdominal symptoms to GI, UTI, or menstrual causes.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Pregnancy possibility changes pregnancy test, ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, urgent gynecology/ED escalation, confidentiality, and documentation.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy and pregnancy-related abdominal or pelvic pain
source.implementation_notes: Do not remove this from adolescent workflows unless a separate reproductive applicability gate supplies equivalent safety coverage.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to pregnancy possibility screening; pregnancy testing and ectopic-symptom pathways are required when pregnancy is possible.

requiredQuestions.label: Scrotal or testicular symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: If male anatomy is possible, is there testicular pain, scrotal swelling, high-riding testis, nausea with scrotal pain, trauma, or pain that started suddenly?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when male anatomy is possible, abdominal pain is lower abdominal, vomiting is prominent, or the child is too young to localize pain reliably.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for testicular torsion or acute scrotum presenting as abdominal pain or vomiting.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive symptoms change urgent scrotal exam, urology/surgical escalation, imaging only if it does not delay care, and transfer planning.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including lower abdominal, pelvic, or testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: Route to the dedicated acute scrotum workup when positive.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this screening row; time-sensitive torsion concern changes action regardless.

conditionalQuestions.label: Appendicitis risk features
conditionalQuestions.text: Are right lower quadrant pain, pain migration, anorexia, fever, pain with cough, pain with hopping, worsening focal tenderness, or prior equivocal ultrasound present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when appendicitis, right lower quadrant pain, focal tenderness, migration, or equivocal imaging is part of the presentation.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Supports pediatric appendicitis risk stratification while preventing risk scores from becoming a standalone rule-out.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change ultrasound-first imaging, serial exam, surgical consultation, observation, and MRI/CT after equivocal ultrasound.

source.source_section: pediatric appendicitis clinical prediction rules and imaging recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Clinical prediction rules may risk-stratify but should not be the sole basis for no advanced imaging in pediatric suspected appendicitis.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this symptom cluster; ACEP summarizes likelihood ratios for imaging strategies, and symptoms alone are not sufficient to rule out appendicitis.

conditionalQuestions.label: Obstruction or intussusception features
conditionalQuestions.text: Are bilious green vomiting, abdominal distension, intermittent severe colicky pain, drawing legs up, pallor or lethargy during episodes, bloody stool, or currant-jelly stool present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when vomiting is bilious, pain is episodic/severe, distension is present, stool is bloody, or obstruction/intussusception is possible.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for bowel obstruction, intussusception, malrotation/volvulus, or other surgical emergency.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change urgent surgical/ED escalation, abdominal imaging, NPO/IV access, analgesia, and transfer.

source.source_section: bilious vomiting and serious causes of vomiting
source.implementation_notes: Bilious vomiting is obstruction until proven otherwise; do not wait for broad workup completion.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this red-flag cluster; guideline action is escalation rather than probabilistic reassurance.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ectopic pregnancy warning features
conditionalQuestions.text: If pregnancy is possible, are pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, dizziness, fainting or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, rectal pressure, urinary symptoms, or passage of tissue present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when pregnancy is possible or adolescent lower abdominal/pelvic symptoms are present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies ectopic pregnancy or pregnancy-related emergency symptoms that overlap with GI/GU complaints.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change pregnancy test urgency, pelvic ultrasound/beta-hCG pathway, gynecology/ED escalation, and transfer.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs of ectopic pregnancy
source.implementation_notes: Symptoms can include GI and urinary complaints; maintain pregnancy-safe routing even when the initial complaint is vomiting.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this symptom list; pregnancy testing and ectopic pathway thresholds change management.

conditionalQuestions.label: Stool microbiology triggers
conditionalQuestions.text: Are blood or mucus in stool, suspected septicemia, immunocompromise, recent foreign travel, no improvement by day 7, diagnostic uncertainty, or outbreak/public-health concern present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when diarrhea is present or enteric infection testing is being considered.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies when stool microbiology or public-health steps are guideline-supported.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive triggers change stool testing, isolation/public-health notification, antimicrobial consideration, and follow-up.

source.source_section: indications for stool microbiology and public health notification
source.implementation_notes: Avoid routine stool testing when none of these triggers are present.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this testing-trigger item; guideline indications determine testing.

conditionalQuestions.label: Testicular torsion warning features
conditionalQuestions.text: If male anatomy is possible, is there sudden testicular pain, scrotal swelling, high-riding testis, nausea/vomiting with scrotal pain, or absent cremasteric reflex reported or observed?
options: nausea or vomiting with scrotal pain
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when testicular/scrotal symptoms are present, lower abdominal pain is unexplained, or vomiting is prominent in a child with male anatomy.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies time-sensitive acute scrotum that can masquerade as abdominal pain.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change urgent urology/surgical consultation and imaging timing; do not delay intervention for routine abdominal workup.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: This is a trigger to use the acute scrotum validated intent or local torsion pathway.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this warning-feature screen; torsion is time-sensitive and management-changing.

requiredExam.label: Inspect abdomen
requiredExam.technique: Observe abdominal contour, distension, visible peristalsis, scars, bruising, hernia bulges, and the child's spontaneous movement before palpation.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric abdominal pain or vomiting workup before palpation.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Distension, obstruction, trauma, hernia, peritonitis behavior, or visible surgical clue.

source.source_section: clinical assessment and examination of acute abdominal pain
source.implementation_notes: Keep inspection separate from vitals and palpation.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for this inspection item; findings trigger obstruction, trauma, hernia, or peritonitis pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Distension, visible peristalsis, bruising, or hernia changes imaging, surgical consultation, analgesia, NPO/IV access, and transfer urgency.
requiredExam.limitations: A normal inspection does not exclude appendicitis, UTI, ectopic pregnancy, or early obstruction.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate bowel sounds
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate the abdomen before deep palpation and document bowel sounds as normal, high-pitched/tinkling, absent, or limited by crying/noise.
findings_options: limited by crying or ambient noise
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when vomiting, distension, obstruction, ileus, or surgical abdomen is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Obstruction, ileus, severe gastroenteritis, or surgical abdomen context.

source.source_section: clinical assessment of acute abdominal pain and investigation guided by likely cause
source.implementation_notes: Document as one bedside maneuver, not as part of a generic focused exam bundle.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for bowel sounds in this module; abnormal findings support but do not prove obstruction or ileus.
requiredExam.management_change: High-pitched or absent sounds with symptoms change obstruction/ileus concern, imaging, observation, and surgical escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Bowel sounds are variable and should not rule out appendicitis or obstruction when other red flags are present.

requiredExam.label: Palpate abdomen for focal tenderness
requiredExam.technique: Palpate gently away from the painful area first, then localize tenderness by quadrant while watching the child's face and guarding response.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every abdominal pain workup after initial observation and analgesia when feasible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Appendicitis, cholecystitis, UTI/suprapubic source, constipation, peritonitis, or other localized pathology.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain examination and repeated examination
source.implementation_notes: Appropriate analgesia should be provided; repeated exam is useful when diagnosis is uncertain.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for this broad palpation item; focal tenderness changes imaging and surgical/urinary pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Focal tenderness changes ultrasound-first appendicitis imaging, urine testing, surgical consult, observation, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Young children may guard or cry nonspecifically; repeat after analgesia and caregiver comfort when feasible.

requiredExam.label: Test rebound tenderness
requiredExam.technique: If tolerated and clinically appropriate, gently assess for rebound or release tenderness; avoid repeated painful testing when peritonitis is already clinically evident.
findings_options: not performed because escalation already indicated
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when peritoneal irritation is suspected and the child can tolerate the maneuver.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Peritonitis, perforation, appendicitis complication, or other surgical abdomen.

source.source_section: red flags, peritonism, and acute abdominal pain examination
source.implementation_notes: Escalate on peritonism; do not over-test if pain or distress makes the maneuver harmful.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available here; rebound is a peritoneal danger sign and should be interpreted with the full exam.
requiredExam.management_change: Rebound tenderness changes urgent surgical/ED escalation, imaging, NPO/IV access, antibiotics consideration, and transfer.
requiredExam.limitations: Absence of rebound does not exclude appendicitis; pain, age, and distress affect reliability.

requiredExam.label: Test capillary refill
requiredExam.technique: Press a warm finger or sternum site for about 5 seconds and document refill time, skin temperature, color, and perfusion context.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when vomiting, diarrhea, fever, poor intake, dehydration, or shock is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Clinical dehydration, shock, sepsis physiology, or poor perfusion.

source.source_section: clinical dehydration and shock features including capillary refill and extremity perfusion
source.implementation_notes: Capillary refill is a bedside perfusion maneuver; blood pressure and heart rate remain baseline safety data.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; use guideline dehydration/shock features and trend.
requiredExam.management_change: Delayed refill or cold/mottled extremities changes rehydration route, sepsis/surgical escalation, monitoring, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Ambient temperature, lighting, skin pigmentation, and site selection affect interpretation; use with mental status and urine output.

requiredExam.label: Measure blood pressure
requiredExam.action: Measure blood pressure with an age-appropriate cuff and document perfusion context, pain, and repeat value if abnormal.

source.source_section: age-adjusted physiologic risk features and shock recognition in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Use local age-specific thresholds; do not apply adult hypotension cutoffs alone.

requiredExam.management_change: Low blood pressure or concerning perfusion changes fluid resuscitation, sepsis/surgical escalation, transfer, and monitoring intensity.
requiredExam.limitations: Pain, anxiety, wrong cuff size, and movement can distort readings; reassess abnormal values.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data; management is threshold- and trajectory-driven.
requiredExam.technique: Measure blood pressure using an appropriately sized cuff after a brief rest period, seated or supine as clinically appropriate.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Hypotension or abnormal perfusion changes concern for shock, dehydration, sepsis, hemorrhage, or ruptured ectopic pregnancy in an adolescent.

requiredExam.label: Measure heart rate
requiredExam.action: Measure heart rate and interpret with age, pain, fever, dehydration, perfusion, and trend.

source.source_section: age-adjusted heart-rate risk features for suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Combine with blood pressure, capillary refill, mental status, urine output, and pain control response.

requiredExam.management_change: Persistent tachycardia changes dehydration treatment, analgesia reassessment, sepsis screening, surgical escalation, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Fever, anxiety, pain, medications, and crying affect rate; trend after analgesia or fluids when feasible.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline clinical data.
requiredExam.technique: Palpate or monitor heart rate for a full interval, noting rate and regularity.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Tachycardia can signal pain, dehydration, sepsis, hemorrhage, shock, or worsening surgical abdomen.

requiredExam.label: Measure respiratory rate
requiredExam.action: Count respiratory rate for a full minute when feasible and compare with age-adjusted thresholds.

source.source_section: age-adjusted respiratory risk features for suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Interpret with oxygen saturation, work of breathing, fever, pain, and bedside glucose/ketone concern.

requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal respiratory rate changes sepsis/DKA mimic screening, oxygen/respiratory evaluation, monitoring, and level of care.
requiredExam.limitations: Crying and pain may elevate respiratory rate; reassess after comfort measures when safe.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data.
requiredExam.technique: Count respiratory rate over a full interval, noting rate and effort.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Tachypnea can signal sepsis, dehydration, pain, metabolic acidosis, pneumonia mimic, or respiratory compromise in vomiting illness.

requiredExam.label: Measure temperature
requiredExam.action: Measure temperature and document method, antipyretic timing, and fever trajectory.

source.source_section: alternative diagnoses when fever accompanies vomiting or diarrhea
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric fever/sepsis pathway when fever plus physiologic risk features or unclear source is present.

requiredExam.management_change: Fever changes source localization, urine testing, stool testing, imaging/antibiotics consideration, sepsis escalation, and safety-netting.
requiredExam.limitations: Temperature alone does not diagnose appendicitis, UTI, gastroenteritis, or sepsis; combine with trajectory and exam.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.
requiredExam.technique: Measure temperature using an appropriate bedside thermometer route.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Fever changes concern for appendicitis complications, UTI/pyelonephritis, gastroenteritis, sepsis, pneumonia mimic, and alternative diagnoses.

requiredExam.label: Measure current weight
requiredExam.action: Measure or verify current weight in kilograms before fluid, antiemetic, analgesic, antibiotic, or transfer dosing decisions.

source.source_section: oral rehydration and fluid replacement by weight
source.implementation_notes: Use measured weight when feasible; emergency care may use institutional weight-estimation tools if treatment cannot wait.

requiredExam.management_change: Weight changes ORS volume, IV fluid planning, medication dosing, antibiotic selection logistics, and transfer handoff.
requiredExam.limitations: Do not delay emergency treatment solely to obtain a measured weight in an unstable child.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as medication-safety and fluid-planning data.
requiredExam.technique: Measure current weight using a calibrated scale.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Pediatric abdominal and vomiting care often uses weight-based fluids and medications; missing weight can cause dosing errors.

requiredExam.label: Determine mental status
requiredExam.action: Document alertness, irritability, lethargy, responsiveness to parent/caregiver, and change from baseline.

source.source_section: dehydration, shock, and alternative diagnoses including altered consciousness
source.implementation_notes: Pair with bedside glucose and sepsis screening when mental status is abnormal.

requiredExam.management_change: Lethargy or altered consciousness changes urgent glucose check, fluids, sepsis pathway, neurologic evaluation, monitoring, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Young age, sleep, pain, fear, and medications can complicate interpretation; use caregiver baseline and repeat assessments.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to mental status as baseline acuity data.
requiredExam.technique: Determine mental status using a brief bedside assessment of alertness, orientation, and responsiveness.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Altered responsiveness changes concern for shock, sepsis, dehydration, hypoglycemia, DKA mimic, meningitis, or intracranial cause of vomiting.

requiredExam.label: Measure intake
requiredExam.action: Document usual intake, last successful oral intake, vomiting frequency, diarrhea frequency, wet nappies or urine output, and last urine.

source.source_section: dehydration risk, reduced urine output, stool/vomit counts, rehydration, and home advice
source.implementation_notes: Make this a documented baseline item rather than a physical exam maneuver.

requiredExam.management_change: Poor intake, persistent vomiting, frequent stools, or reduced urine output changes rehydration route, reassessment timing, admission, and caregiver instructions.
requiredExam.limitations: Caregiver estimates may be imprecise; trend and objective urine/stool counts when possible.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to intake/output as baseline safety data.
requiredExam.technique: Determine fluid intake from bedside records or direct measurement.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Intake and urine output change dehydration severity, oral versus IV/enteral fluids, admission, and safety-netting.

requiredExam.label: Measure urine output
requiredExam.action: Document usual intake, last successful oral intake, vomiting frequency, diarrhea frequency, wet nappies or urine output, and last urine.
findings_options: Unable to obtain urine output history

source.source_section: dehydration risk, reduced urine output, stool/vomit counts, rehydration, and home advice
source.implementation_notes: Make this a documented baseline item rather than a physical exam maneuver.

requiredExam.management_change: Poor intake, persistent vomiting, frequent stools, or reduced urine output changes rehydration route, reassessment timing, admission, and caregiver instructions.
requiredExam.limitations: Caregiver estimates may be imprecise; trend and objective urine/stool counts when possible.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to intake/output as baseline safety data.
requiredExam.technique: Determine urine output from bedside records, wet nappies/diapers, or direct measurement.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Intake and urine output change dehydration severity, oral versus IV/enteral fluids, admission, and safety-netting.

requiredExam.label: Measure bedside glucose
requiredExam.action: Measure point-of-care glucose when the child is unwell, altered, dehydrated, has persistent vomiting without diarrhea, polyuria/polydipsia, weight loss, diabetes, or acidosis concern.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis and need to consider serious non-gastroenteritis causes
source.implementation_notes: If glucose is abnormal, route to the hyperglycemia/DKA or hypoglycemia local pathway rather than treating as isolated GI illness.

requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal bedside glucose changes immediate dextrose/insulin/ketone/acidosis workup, fluid plan, monitoring, and level of care.
requiredExam.limitations: A normal glucose does not exclude dehydration, sepsis, surgical abdomen, or intracranial causes of vomiting.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to bedside glucose as baseline safety data.
requiredExam.technique: Measure bedside glucose using point-of-care testing.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Pediatric abdominal pain, vomiting, or acute abdomen is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Hypoglycemia and hyperglycemic crisis can present with vomiting or abdominal pain and change immediate management.

conditionalExam.label: Percuss abdomen for peritoneal irritation
conditionalExam.technique: Gently percuss each quadrant or use cough/hop observation in an age-appropriate way to assess pain from peritoneal irritation.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when localized, movement-related, or peritoneal pain is suspected and the child can tolerate it.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Peritoneal irritation from appendicitis, perforation, or other surgical abdomen.

source.source_section: acute abdominal pain assessment and surgical red flags
source.implementation_notes: Use age-appropriate maneuver and avoid repeated painful testing.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for this conditional maneuver; positive findings change escalation.
conditionalExam.management_change: Pain with percussion, cough, or hop changes surgical consultation, ultrasound-first imaging, observation, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.limitations: Negative testing does not rule out appendicitis; reliability depends on age, pain, and cooperation.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect scrotum
conditionalExam.technique: With consent and privacy, inspect for swelling, erythema, asymmetry, high-riding testis, trauma, or visible hernia when male anatomy is possible.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when scrotal/testicular symptoms are present, male anatomy is possible, or lower abdominal pain/vomiting is unexplained.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Testicular torsion, acute scrotum, incarcerated hernia, trauma, or referred abdominal pain source.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including lower abdominal, pelvic, or testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: If torsion is possible, route urgently to acute scrotum pathway.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in this module; time-sensitive torsion concern should drive urgent action.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal inspection changes urgent urology/surgical escalation and avoids delayed abdominal-only workup.
conditionalExam.limitations: A normal inspection does not exclude torsion; use dedicated acute scrotum workup if symptoms persist.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate testis position
conditionalExam.technique: With consent and privacy, gently palpate testis lie and position, comparing sides when possible and stopping if pain is severe.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when torsion or acute scrotum is possible and examination will not delay urgent care.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Testicular torsion, acute scrotal pathology, or hernia-related pain.

source.source_section: vomiting differential diagnosis including testicular pain
source.implementation_notes: Avoid false reassurance; escalate when torsion remains possible.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available here; use dedicated acute scrotum pathway for evidence-specific exam interpretation.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal lie or high-riding position changes urgent urology/surgical consultation and transfer urgency.
conditionalExam.limitations: Pain, age, and consent can limit exam; do not delay escalation for a perfect exam.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect oral mucosa
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect lips, tongue, and oral mucosa for moisture, tears when crying, and ability to tolerate oral fluids.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when dehydration, poor intake, vomiting, diarrhea, or reduced urine output is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Clinical dehydration and oral rehydration feasibility.

source.source_section: clinical dehydration and rehydration assessment
source.implementation_notes: Use with intake/output, capillary refill, mental status, and weight.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available; dehydration findings change rehydration and disposition.
conditionalExam.management_change: Dry mucosa, absent tears, or inability to drink changes ORS supervision, NG/IV fluids, observation, and admission threshold.
conditionalExam.limitations: Mucosa can be misleading after drinking or mouth breathing; interpret with urine output and perfusion.

clinical_cutoff_review.review_note: Cutoff rows were added because the prior module evidence did not expose enough explicit guideline thresholds for pathway traversal.
